steps.title: Extract histogram data and locate original median values
steps.content:
From the histogram, the frequency distribution is:
  • [10, 15): 3 salmon
  • [15, 20): 6 salmon
  • [20, 25): 2 salmon
  • [25, 30): 1 salmon
  • [30, 35): 2 salmon
Total: 14 salmon. With 14 values, the median is the average of the 7th and 8th values. Counting cumulatively: positions 1–3 fall in [10, 15); positions 4–9 fall in [15, 20). Both the 7th and 8th values are in [15, 20).

steps.title: Test whether the median must increase
steps.content:
After adding 85 pounds, we have 15 values. The new median is the 8th value (the middle value of 15). This 8th value comes from the same location in the [15, 20) range—adding 85 at the extreme end does not change it.

Original median = (7th + 8th) ÷ 2
New median = 8th value

Is the new median greater? Only if 8th > 7th. If 7th = 8th (possible, since the histogram does not specify exact values), then new equals original. The median is not guaranteed to increase. Statement I is not guaranteed.

steps.title: Test whether the mean must increase
steps.content:
Original mean: $$\frac{S}{14}$$ where $S$ is the sum of the original 14 weights.

New mean: $$\frac{S + 85}{15}$$

The new mean exceeds the original mean when:
$$\frac{S + 85}{15} > \frac{S}{14}$$

Cross-multiply and simplify:
$$14(S + 85) > 15S$$
$$14S + 1190 > 15S$$
$$S < 1190$$

The maximum possible sum from the histogram is $S_{\text{max}} = 3(15) + 6(20) + 2(25) + 1(30) + 2(35) = 315$. Since $315 < 1190$, the inequality $S < 1190$ always holds, so the mean always increases. Statement II is guaranteed. That matches choice B — B is correct.
